extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true by exactly one rule: rec_a_from_conjunction (priority 50), which fires only when g6pd_deficiency AND diabetes AND low_ejection_fraction are all true; otherwise rec_drug_a defaults to false. So the only way to get rec_drug_a=true is that exact three-way conjunction. In precisely that same condition, two rules match rec_drug_b: guard_suppress_b (priority 90, sets false) with trigger (g6pd AND diabetes AND low_EF), and rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50, sets true) with trigger g6pd. Per-output conflict resolution takes the highest priority, so priority 90 (false) overrides priority 50 (true), forcing rec_drug_b=false whenever the conjunction holds. Therefore rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b can never both be true. hyperkalemia only affects rec_drug_c and is irrelevant. The guard rule's trigger is identical to rec_a's trigger and has strictly higher priority, so the mutual-exclusion property holds across the entire 16-point input space.
